EDCS-11200898.translation: With our lords the Augusti Philippi safe, the fortunate legion VII Claudia Pia Fidelis, your six centuries of the former and latter hastati, devoted to their divine will and majesty. RNS-
EDCS-11200898.summary: This inscription records the devotion of six centuries of Legion VII Claudia Pia Fidelis to the reigning emperors, likely Philip the Arab and his son Philip II, during the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11200902.text_edition: Soli Invicto Mithrae librarii cum Antonio Zoilo actuario praepositi numeri Surorum posuerunt
EDCS-11200902.translation: To Sol Invictus Mithras, the scribes, along with Antonius Zoilius, the actuary in charge of the numerus of Syrians, placed this.
EDCS-11200902.summary: This is a dedication to Sol Invictus Mithras by scribes and an actuary associated with a military unit of Syrians (numerus Surorum), likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11200904.text_edition: Dardanicae us decurio coloniae et Aelius  decurio? coloniae ex voto posuerunt
EDCS-11200904.translation: Dardanicae, a decurion of the colony, and Aelius, a decurion of the colony, placed this in accordance with a vow.
EDCS-11200904.summary: This inscription records a joint votive dedication by two decurions from a colony, named Dardanicae and Aelius.

EDCS-11200905.translation: I, Aurelius Dardanus, placed this vow.
EDCS-11200905.summary: This is a short votive inscription by an individual named Aurelius Dardanus, indicating the fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-11200910.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Aelio Iulio Iuliano decurioni quaestoricio aedilicio coloniae Romulae Valeria Gemellina marito bene merenti posuit coniugi pro meritis quondam karissimo coniunx hanc Iuliano domum flendo fabricavi perennem frigida qua membra possint requiescere morti quattuor hic denos vixit sine culpa per annos et sua perfunctus vidit cum gloria honores ecce Gemellina pietate ducta marito struxi dolens digno sedem cum liberis una inter pampinea virgulta et gramina laeta umbra super rami virides ubi densa ministrat qui legis hos versus opta leve terra viator
EDCS-11200910.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Aelius Iulius Iulianus, decurion, quaestor, aedile of the colony Romula, Valeria Gemellina placed this for her well-deserving husband. To my once dearest husband, for his merits, I, his spouse, weeping, built this eternal home for Iulianus, where his cold limbs may rest in death. Here he lived for forty years without fault, and having performed his duties, he saw honors with glory. Behold, I, Gemellina, moved by piety, sorrowfully built this seat for my worthy husband, together with our children, amidst the vine tendrils and joyous grasses, where the dense green shade of the branches provides cover. You who read these verses, traveler, wish him light earth.
EDCS-11200910.summary: This is a detailed and poignant funerary inscription from Valeria Gemellina to her deceased husband, Aelius Iulius Iulianus, a decurion, quaestor, and aedile of Colonia Romula, commemorating his life and her sorrow, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11200936.translation: Marinus Summus, in front of the brick.
EDCS-11200936.summary: This short and cryptic inscription appears to be a brick stamp or a mason's mark, indicating the name Marinus Summus and a location relative to a brick.

EDCS-11200958.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, preserving part of a name, Sextus Cornelius, likely from a tombstone or a dedication.

EDCS-11201144.translation: Transcribed and verified, made from the document which had been posted at Alburnus Maior at the Resculum station, in which was written that which is written below: Artemidorus, son of Apollonius, master of the college of Jupiter Cernenus, and Valerius, son of Nikon, and Offas, son of Menophilus, quaestors of the same college, having publicly posted this document, attest that from the above-mentioned college, where there were 54 men, no more than 17 men remained at Alburnus, and that Iulius, son of Iulius, also their co-master, from the day of his magistracy, has not come to Alburnus, nor to the college, nor has he rendered account to those who were present, and if any of them had had, he would have rendered it either for funerals and he would have received his surety in which he had guaranteed them. And now, however, they are neither sufficient for funeral expenses, nor do they have a burial place, nor has anyone for such a long time, on the days when it is prescribed by law, wished to convene or contribute funeral expenses or gifts. And therefore, they publicly attest through this document that if anyone dies, he should not think he has a college or that he will have any claim for a funeral from them. Posted at Alburnus Maior on the 5th Ides of February (February 9th), with Imperator Lucius Aurelius Verus for the third time and Quadratus as consuls. Done at Alburnus Maior. Transcribed and verified, made from the document which had been posted at Alburnus Maior at the Resculum station, in which was written that which is written below: Artemidorus, son of Apollonius, master of the college of Jupiter Cernenus, and Valerius, son of Nikon, and Offas, son of Menophilus, quaestors of the same college, having publicly posted this document, attest that from the above-mentioned college, where there were 54 men, no more than 17 men remained at Alburnus, namely: Lucius Vasidius Victor, Gaius Secundinus Legitimus, Stertinius Rusticus, Aelius Plator, Geldo, Ulpius Felix, September Plator. And that Iulius, son of Iulius, also their co-master, from the day of his magistracy, has not come to Alburnus, nor to the college, nor has he rendered account to those who were present, and if any of them had had, he would have rendered it either for funerals and he would have received his surety in which he had guaranteed them. And now, however, they are neither sufficient for funeral expenses, nor do they have a burial place, nor has anyone for such a long time, on the days when it is prescribed by law, wished to convene or contribute funeral expenses or gifts. And therefore, they publicly attest through this document that if anyone dies, he should not think he has a college or that he will have any claim for a funeral from them. Posted at Alburnus Maior on the 5th Ides of February (February 9th), with Imperator Lucius Aurelius Verus for the third time and Quadratus as consuls. Done at Alburnus Maior.
EDCS-11201144.summary: This extensive inscription is a public notice, likely a bronze tablet, recording a declaration from 167 AD by the master and quaestors of a funerary college dedicated to Jupiter Cernenus at Alburnus Maior, announcing the critical decline in membership and the failure of members to pay dues, thus disclaiming responsibility for future funeral benefits.

EDCS-11201145.translation: Epicadus... in which... of Cerdo(?), until all... done at Alburnus Maior on the 15th day before the Kalends of October, in the consulship of Quintillus and Priscus. On the 15th day before the Kalends of October, Andueia, daughter of Bato, by surety... yielded... Apulus Cerialis... Hel(i)ci... of Felicis(?) son of Pici... Maximus Veranis, Servilius Cassi(?), of Epicadus... debtor of Iulius Macedo, surety.
EDCS-11201145.summary: This inscription records a complex legal transaction, likely involving a debt, surety, and possibly a transfer of property, dated to September 17, 159 AD at Alburnus Maior, with various individuals listed as parties or witnesses.

EDCS-11201146.translation: One hundred and forty denarii of principal and their interest, at one hundredth per day for as long as she abstains, that both these things be given honestly and correctly, Anduenna, daughter of Bato, requested a surety. Iulius Alexander promised to give by his surety, which she will have to return on the day she requests, with the above-written interest. That both these things, principal and interest, be given honestly and correctly, the aforesaid Anduenna requested a surety. Iulius Alexander promised to give by his surety. Done at Deusara, on the 12th day before the Kalends of July, in the consulship of Rusticus II and Aquilinus. One hundred and forty denarii of principal and their interest from that day, at one hundredth per day, that both these things be given honestly and correctly, Anduenna, daughter of Bato, requested a surety. Iulius Alexander promised to give by his surety, which she will have to return on the day she requests, with the above-written interest. That both these things, principal and interest, be given honestly and correctly, the aforesaid Anduenna requested a surety. Iulius Alexander promised to give by his surety. Done at Deusara, on the 12th day before the Kalends of July, in the consulship of Rusticus II and Aquilinus. Of Iulius Max(imus?), of Lucius(?), of Aurelius S(everus?), of Verus(?), of Vasidius(?)... of Nicomedes(?)... one hundred and forty denarii. Iulius Alexander... one hundred and forty denarii for Iulius Alexander.
EDCS-11201146.summary: This diptych (a pair of tablets) records a loan agreement of 140 denarii with daily interest between Anduenna daughter of Bato and Iulius Alexander, made on June 20, 162 AD at Deusara, with a list of additional witnesses or related individuals.

EDCS-11201148.translation: Sixty denarii, which on the day he requests, are to be given honestly and correctly, Iulius Alexander requested a surety. Alexander Caricci promised to give by surety and said that he had received as a loan and owed these 60 denarii, as written above, and their interest from this day for 30 days, to Iulius Alexander or to whom that matter pertains, Iulius Alexander requested a surety. Alexander Caricci promised to give by surety. Titius Primitius ordered this to be by his surety on the above-written day, that the principal with interest be paid correctly and honestly. Done at Alburnus Maior, on the 13th day before the Kalends of November, in the consulship of Rusticus II and Aquilinus. Sixty denarii, which on the day he requests, are to be given honestly and correctly, Iulius Alexander requested a surety. Alexander Caricci promised to give by surety and said that he had received as a loan and owed these 60 denarii, as written above, and their interest. Of Lucius Vasidius Victor, of... Batonis Preuvi(?), of Tovetis, Titius Primitius, of Alexander Caricci himself, the debtor.
EDCS-11201148.summary: This diptych documents a loan of 60 denarii from Iulius Alexander to Alexander Caricci, with a specific interest term and surety provided by Titius Primitius, dated to October 20, 162 AD at Alburnus Maior, listing several witnesses.

EDCS-11201149.translation: Maximus, son of Bato, purchased and took ownership of a girl named Passia, or whatever other name she has, approximately six years old, bought as a 'sportellaria' (likely a slave sold at market or by auction). He bought her from Dasius, son of Verzo, a Pirustan from Kavieretium, for two hundred and five denarii. The girl is healthy, free from thefts and harms, not a runaway or a wanderer. He guarantees that if anyone evicts this girl, or any part of her, preventing Maximus, son of Bato, or to whom this matter pertains, from rightfully holding and possessing her, then he shall pay twice the purchase price. Maximus, son of Bato, requested a surety. Dasius, son of Verzo, a Pirustan from Kavieretium, promised by his surety. Dasius, son of Verzo, stated that he had received and possessed two hundred and five denarii from Maximus, son of Bato, for the above-written girl. Done at Carto, on the 16th day before the Kalends of April, in the consulship of Titus Aelius Caesar Antoninus Pius II and Bruttius Praesens II. Maximus, son of Bato, purchased and took ownership of a girl named Passia, or whatever other name she has, approximately six years old, bought as a 'sportellaria'. He bought her from Dasius, son of Verzo, a Pirustan from Kavieretium, for two hundred and five denarii. The girl is healthy, free from thefts and harms, not a runaway or a wanderer. He guarantees that if anyone evicts this girl, or any part of her, preventing Maximus, son of Bato, or to whom this matter pertains, from rightfully holding and possessing her, then he shall pay twice the purchase price. Maximus, son of Bato, requested a surety. Dasius, son of Verzo, a Pirustan from Kavieretium, promised by his surety. Dasius, son of Verzo, stated that he had received and possessed two hundred and five denarii from Maximus, son of Bato, for the above-written girl. Done at Carto, on the 16th day before the Kalends of April, in the consulship of Titus Aelius Caesar Antoninus Pius II and Bruttius Praesens II. Of Maximus Veneti, princeps. Of Masurius Messius, decurion. Of Annesis Andunocnetis. Of Planus Verzonis Sclaietis. Of Liccai Epicadi Marciniesi. Of Epicadus Marciniesi. Of Epicadus Plarentis, who is also Mico. Of Dasius Verzonis himself, the seller.
EDCS-11201149.summary: This diptych records the sale and purchase of a six-year-old slave girl named Passia for 205 denarii, by Maximus son of Bato from Dasius son of Verzo, a Pirustan, including guarantees about her health and status, dated March 17, 139 AD at Carto, with a comprehensive list of witnesses.

EDCS-11201150.translation: Dasius Breucus purchased and took ownership of a boy, Apalaustus, or whatever other name he has, of Greek nationality, 'apochatum' (likely a specific type of slave), for two 'unciae' (shares), for 600 denarii, from Bellicus, son of Alexander. Marcus Vibius Longus was requested as surety. The boy is guaranteed to be healthy, free from thefts and harms, not a wanderer, runaway, or sickly. And if anyone evicts this boy, or any part of him, preventing the above-written buyer or to whom this matter pertains from rightfully using, enjoying, holding, and possessing him, then whatever amount is thus evicted from him, that double amount of money is to be given honestly and correctly. Dasius Breucus requested a surety. Bellicus, son of Alexander, promised to give by surety. The same Vibius Longus ordered this to be by his surety. Bellicus, son of Alexander, stated that he had received and possessed 600 denarii from Dasius Breucus as the price for the above-written boy. Done at the camp of Legion XIII Gemina, on the 17th day before the Kalends of June, in the consulship of Rufinus and Quadratus. Dasius Breucus purchased and took ownership of a boy, Apalaustus, or whatever other name he has, of Greek nationality, 'apochatum' for two 'unciae', for 600 denarii, from Bellicus, son of Alexander. Marcus Vibius Longus was requested as surety. The boy is guaranteed to be healthy, free from thefts and harms, not a wanderer, runaway, or sickly. And if anyone evicts this boy, or any part of him, preventing the above-written buyer or to whom this matter pertains from rightfully using, enjoying, holding, and possessing him, then whatever amount is thus evicted from him, that double amount of money is to be given honestly and correctly. Dasius Breucus requested a surety. Bellicus, son of Alexander, promised to give by surety. The same Marcus Vibius Longus ordered this to be by his surety. Bellicus, son of Alexander, stated that he had received and possessed 600 denarii from Dasius Breucus as the price for the above-written boy.
EDCS-11201150.summary: This diptych records the sale and purchase of a Greek slave boy named Apalaustus for 600 denarii by Dasius Breucus from Bellicus son of Alexander, with a guarantee of the boy's health and status, dated May 16, 167 AD at the camp of Legion XIII Gemina, and Marcus Vibius Longus as surety.

EDCS-11201152.translation: Andueia, daughter of Bato, purchased and took ownership of half of a house, the right part as one enters, which is in Alburnus Maior, in the village of the Pirustae, between the neighbors Plator Acceptianus and Ingenuus, son of Callistus, for three hundred denarii, from Veturius Valens. This half-share of the house, with its fences, enclosures, boundaries, access ways, locks, and windows, just as it is fixed by nail and is best and greatest, she is to hold rightfully. And if anyone evicts this house, or any part of it, preventing Andueia, daughter of Bato, or to whom this matter pertains, from rightfully holding, possessing, and enjoying it, if it is not thus permitted, then that amount of money is to be given honestly. Andueia, daughter of Bato, requested a surety. Veturius Valens promised by surety. Veturius Valens stated that he had received and possessed the price of three hundred denarii for this half-share of the house from Andueia, daughter of Bato. And it was agreed between them that Veturius Valens shall pay the taxes for that house up to the census. Done at Alburnus Maior, the day before the Nones of May, in the consulship of Quintillus and Priscus. Andueia, daughter of Bato, purchased and took ownership of half of a house, the right part as one enters, which is in Alburnus Maior, in the village of the Pirustae, between the neighbors Plator Acceptianus and Ingenuus, son of Callistus, and any other neighbors, and the public road, for three hundred denarii, from Veturius Valens. This house, as described, with its fences, enclosures, boundaries, access ways, locks, and windows, just as it is fixed by nail and is best and greatest, Andueia, daughter of Bato, is to hold rightfully. And if anyone evicts this house, or half of it, or any part of it, preventing Andueia, daughter of Bato, or to whom this matter pertains, from rightfully holding, possessing, and enjoying it, if it is not thus permitted, then whatever amount it is that thus cannot be held and possessed, that amount of money is to be given honestly. Andueia, daughter of Bato, requested a surety. Veturius Valens promised to give by surety. Veturius Valens stated that he had received and possessed the price of three hundred denarii for this half-share of the house from Andueia, daughter of Bato. And it was agreed between them that Veturius Valens shall pay the taxes for that house up to the census. Done at Alburnus Maior, the day before the Nones of May, in the consulship of Quintillus and Priscus. Lucius Vasidius Victor signed. Of Titus Flavius Felix. Of Marcus Lucanus Melior. Of Plator Carpus. Of Titus Aurelius Priscus. Of Bato Annaeus. Of Veturius Valens, the seller.
EDCS-11201152.summary: This diptych records the sale of a half-share of a house in Alburnus Maior for 300 denarii by Veturius Valens to Andueia, daughter of Bato, including property descriptions, guarantees against eviction, and an agreement on tax payments, dated May 6, 159 AD, with a list of witnesses.

EDCS-11201153.translation: In the consulship of Laelianus and Pastor, on the 10th (?) day before the Kalends of November, I, Adiutor Macari, wrote, requested in the presence of Lucius Ulpius Valerius himself, because he stated that he could not read or write, that which he said he leased and leased to Socratio, son of Socras, his services from this day until the Ides of the following year, for one year, for seventy (?) denarii; the wages will be paid at their proper times. These services he must perform in a healthy and capable manner. If the employer impedes... he will owe... to withdraw or cease... ceased... done at Alburnus Maior (?).
EDCS-11201153.summary: This incomplete inscription records a labor contract where Lucius Ulpius Valerius leased his services to Socratio son of Socras for one year for 70 denarii, dated to October 23, 163 AD, and drawn up by Adiutor Macari in Alburnus Maior.

EDCS-11201154.translation: In the consulship of Macrinus and Celsus, on the 13th day before the Kalends of June, I, Flavius Secundinus, wrote, requested by Memmius Asclepius because he stated that he could not read or write, that which he said he leased and leased his services in gold work to Aurelius Adiutor from this day until the Ides of November next, for seventy denarii, and for his children (?), denarii, and for children(?), he must receive wages during these times. These services he must perform in a healthy and capable manner for the aforesaid employer. If he wishes to withdraw or cease against the employer's will, he must pay five sesterces per day to the employer. If a flu-like illness impedes the work, the employer must compute pro rata. If, after the time has elapsed, the employer delays paying the wages, he shall be held by the same penalty, with the exception of three ceased days. Done at Immenosus Maior. Titus Beusantis, who is also Bradua. Socratio Socrationis. Memmius Asclepius.
EDCS-11201154.summary: This inscription records a labor contract where Memmius Asclepius, unable to read or write, leased his services as a goldsmith to Aurelius Adiutor for a period from May 20 to November 13, 164 AD, for 70 denarii, detailing terms for payment, penalties for breach, and exceptions for illness, done at Immenosus Maior.

EDCS-11201156.translation: In the consulship of Verus III and Quadratus, on the 4th day before the Kalends of June, Lupus, son of Carens, stated that he had received and did receive fifty denarii entrusted to him by Iulius Alexander, which he must return to him without any dispute. Done at Alburnus Maior.
EDCS-11201156.summary: This inscription records a formal acknowledgment by Lupus son of Carens that he received 50 denarii entrusted to him by Iulius Alexander, which he is obliged to return without dispute, dated May 29, 167 AD and executed at Alburnus Maior.

EDCS-11201157.translation: Between Cassius Frontinus and Iulius Alexander, a money-lending partnership was agreed upon, from the 10th day before the Kalends of January last, in the consulship of Pudens and Pollio, until the day before the Ides of April next, that whatever profit or loss arises from that partnership, they must bear in equal portions. In this partnership, Iulius Alexander contributed five hundred denarii in cash or in produce, and Secundus, slave of Cassius Palumbus, contributed two hundred denarii... sixty-seven... he will owe... in which partnership, if anyone is found to have committed fraud with malicious intent, he must contribute one denarius for every as, one denarius... in addition... and when the time has elapsed, after deducting the debt, they must either reclaim the above-written sum or divide any surplus. Cassius Frontinus stipulated that this be given, done, and provided. Iulius Alexander promised. Regarding this matter, two pairs of sealed tablets were signed. Also, 50 denarii are owed to Lossa, which she must receive from the aforesaid partners. Done at Deusara, on the 5th day before the Kalends of April, in the consulship of Verus III and Quadratus. Between Cassius Frontinus and Iulius Alexander, a money-lending partnership...
EDCS-11201157.summary: This diptych records the formation of a money-lending partnership between Cassius Frontinus and Iulius Alexander, effective from December 23, 161 AD to April 12, 167 AD, with details on profit/loss sharing, initial contributions, penalties for fraud, and a debt owed to Lossa, executed at Deusara on March 28, 167 AD.

EDCS-11201161.translation: When Pontianus and Rufinus were consuls, on the 7th day before the Ides of February (February 7), Valerius Firmus wrote, having been asked by Verzo, son of Beusans, because... son of Beusans, by right... agricultural land... which... his own... Dasius, son of Verzo... twenty denarii... agricultural land of Beusans... to Alburnus Maior...
EDCS-11201161.summary: This fragmentary inscription, dated to February 7 of the year when Pontianus and Rufinus were consuls (likely 260 AD), records a legal transaction involving Valerius Firmus, Verzo son of Beusans, Dasius son of Verzo, and agricultural land, possibly concerning a sale or transfer.

EDCS-11300117.summary: This brief inscription records the presence of the Legio V Macedonica, with an unclear abbreviation following.

EDCS-11300118.summary: This short inscription attests to the Legio V Macedonica and mentions a person named Varinia, possibly a member of the legion or someone associated with it.

EDCS-11300123.summary: This brief inscription is a dedication to Varinia Dalmata, likely from a tombstone or monument.

EDCS-11300180.text_edition: Dis Manibus Umbria Africana vixit annis LXV  Umbrius Villanus matri pientissimae ponendum curavit
EDCS-11300180.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Umbria Africana lived for 65 years. Umbrius Villanus took care to place this for his most devoted mother.
EDCS-11300180.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument dedicated by Umbrius Villanus to his most devoted mother, Umbria Africana, who lived to be 65 years old.

EDCS-11300181.text_edition: Dis Manibus Caius Ianius Ianuarius vixit annis XLI Ianius Marcianus et Ianiae Ianuariae ipsi patri bene merenti et C Marcia coniux pia cum FILIVIO suam posuerunt ex C
EDCS-11300181.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Ianius Ianuarius lived for 41 years. Ianius Marcianus and Iania Ianuaria, for their well-deserving father himself, and his devoted wife Marcia with her son, placed this.
EDCS-11300181.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Gaius Ianius Ianuarius, who lived 41 years, and was set up by his children Ianius Marcianus and Iania Ianuaria, along with his devoted wife Marcia and their son, for their well-deserving father.

EDCS-11300183.text_edition: RA ICA X  vixit  Aeli- Sa in dio et B parvae V T L
EDCS-11300183.translation: RA ICA X lived. Aeli Sa in deo and B parvae V T L.
EDCS-11300183.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to be a funerary dedication, possibly for individuals whose names are partially preserved as Aeli Sa and B parvae, and mentions a lifespan of ten years.

EDCS-11300184.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute Imperatorum Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis et Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti et Publi Septimi Getaes sic S Caius Valerius Ingenuus signifer legionis XIII geminae ex voto
EDCS-11300184.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, for the welfare of the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and Publius Septimius Geta, Gaius Valerius Ingenuus, standard-bearer of the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, fulfilling a vow, placed this.
EDCS-11300184.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication made to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus by Gaius Valerius Ingenuus, a standard-bearer of Legio XIII Gemina, for the health and welfare of the imperial family (Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta), made in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-11300185.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus. Aurelius Praesentinus and...
EDCS-11300185.summary: This fragmentary inscription is a dedication to Silvanus Domesticus, a domestic god of the woods and fields, made by Aurelius Praesentinus and possibly others.

EDCS-11300191.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Primin ONCA vixit annos V item filia VX CO et IPT AVD
EDCS-11300191.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Primin ONCA lived for 5 years. Also his daughter VX CO and IPT AVD.
EDCS-11300191.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription commemorates Primin ONCA, who lived for five years, and also mentions his daughter and another individual or individuals with partially preserved names.

EDCS-11300193.text_edition: Numini domini nostri Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti Caius Gaurius Gaurianus sacerdos coloniae Apulensis et Flavius Sotericus Augustalis coloniae Sarmizegetusae conductores ferrariarum
EDCS-11300193.translation: To the divine power of our lord Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Gaius Gaurius Gaurianus, priest of the colony of Apulum, and Flavius Sotericus, Augustalis of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, contractors of the iron mines [dedicated this].
EDCS-11300193.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Caracalla by two prominent officials, one a priest from Apulum and the other an Augustalis from Sarmizegetusa, who were also contractors of iron mines, likely around the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300201.text_edition: Pro salute ddominorum nnostrorum invictissimorum Impperatorum Severi et Antonini et Getae Caesaris Augggustorum sic et Iuliae et Plautillae Auggustarum et Plautiani clarissimi viri praefecti praetorio patris Augustae sub Pomponio Liberale consulari Mauri Micienses et Iulius Euangelianus praefectus templum deorum patriorum vetustate conlapsum sua pecunia et opera restituerunt Cilone II et Libone consulibus
EDCS-11300201.translation: For the health of our most unconquered lords, the Emperors Severus and Antoninus and Geta Caesar Augustus, and also Julia Augusta and Plautilla Augusta, and Plautianus, most illustrious man, praetorian prefect, father of the Augusta, under Pomponius Liberalis, consular legate, the Mauri Micienses and Julius Evangelianus, prefect, restored the temple of the ancestral gods, which had collapsed from old age, with their own money and effort, during the consulship of Cilo II and Libo.
EDCS-11300201.summary: This inscription documents the restoration of a dilapidated temple dedicated to ancestral gods by the Mauri Micienses and Julius Evangelianus, during the joint reign of Septimius Severus and his sons, dated precisely to 204 AD by the consular dating.

EDCS-11300202.text_edition: Deo Invicto Publius Aelius Euphorus pro salute sua et suorum templum a solo fecit
EDCS-11300202.translation: To the Unconquered God, Publius Aelius Euphorus, for his own health and that of his family, built this temple from the ground up.
EDCS-11300202.summary: This inscription records the construction of a temple to an Unconquered God by Publius Aelius Euphorus, dedicating it for the well-being of himself and his family.

EDCS-11300207.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Commageno Iulius Trophimus vovit
EDCS-11300207.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus Commagenus, Julius Trophimus vowed.
EDCS-11300207.summary: This inscription records a vow made by Julius Trophimus to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, specifically identified with the epithets Dolichenus and Commagenus, indicating a syncretic cult.

EDCS-11300210.text_edition: In honorem domus divinae Genio Miciae Marcus Cornelius Stratonicus Augustalis coloniae Dacicae Sarmizegetusae pecunia sua dedit
EDCS-11300210.translation: In honor of the divine house, to the Genius of Micia, Marcus Cornelius Stratonicus, Augustalis of the Dacic colony of Sarmizegetusa, gave this with his own money.
EDCS-11300210.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Marcus Cornelius Stratonicus, an Augustalis from Sarmizegetusa, to the Genius of Micia, demonstrating local piety and support for the imperial house.

EDCS-11300211.text_edition: Pro salute domus divinae sacrum Genium sic Miciae Marcus Cornelius Stratonicus Augustalis coloniae et ante frontem lapidibus stravit
EDCS-11300211.translation: For the health of the divine house, Marcus Cornelius Stratonicus, Augustalis of the colony, dedicated this sacred Genius of Micia and paved the area in front with stones.
EDCS-11300211.summary: This inscription records Marcus Cornelius Stratonicus, an Augustalis of the colony, dedicating a sacred Genius of Micia for the health of the imperial family and also undertaking the paving of the area in front of it.

EDCS-11300213.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo veterani et cives Romani per Caium Antonium Crispinum magistrum pagi Miciensis
EDCS-11300213.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the veterans and Roman citizens through Gaius Antonius Crispinus, master of the pagus Miciensis.
EDCS-11300213.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by veterans and Roman citizens, facilitated by Gaius Antonius Crispinus, the master of the pagus Miciensis.

EDCS-11300214.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo veterani et cives Romani Micienses per Aurelium Alpinum et Claudium Nicomaedem sic magistros votum solverunt libentes merito
EDCS-11300214.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the veterans and Roman citizens of Micia, through Aurelius Alpinus and Claudius Nicomaedes, masters, willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-11300214.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by the veterans and Roman citizens of Micia, managed by their masters Aurelius Alpinus and Claudius Nicomaedes.

EDCS-11300218.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Claudius Saecularis beneficiarius consularis pro se et Priscino collega votum solvit
EDCS-11300218.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Claudius Saecularis, beneficiarius consularis, for himself and his colleague Priscinus, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300218.summary: This inscription records Claudius Saecularis, a beneficiarius consularis, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for himself and his colleague Priscinus, likely a military dedication from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300219.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Iulius Zoticus ex voto posuit
EDCS-11300219.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Gaius Julius Zoticus placed this in accordance with his vow.
EDCS-11300219.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Gaius Julius Zoticus to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, made in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-11300220.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Mateus Victor veteranus
EDCS-11300220.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Marcus Mateus Victor, a veteran [dedicated this].
EDCS-11300220.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Marcus Mateus Victor, a veteran, to Jupiter Best and Greatest.

EDCS-11300221.text_edition: Iovi Hierapolitano fecit Cassius Rufus votum dedit
EDCS-11300221.translation: Cassius Rufus made this and gave his vow to Jupiter of Hierapolis.
EDCS-11300221.summary: This inscription records a fulfilled vow and dedication by Cassius Rufus to Jupiter of Hierapolis.

EDCS-11300226.text_edition: Libero Patri et Liberae Ulpius Abascantus votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300226.translation: To Liber Pater and Libera, Ulpius Abascantus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300226.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Ulpius Abascantus to Liber Pater and Libera.

EDCS-11300229.text_edition: Minervae Augustae et Genio cohortis II Flaviae Commagenorum Ianuarius actarius  votum libens merito s(olvit)
EDCS-11300229.translation: To Minerva Augusta and the Genius of the Second Flavian Cohort of Commagenians, Ianuarius, the accountant, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300229.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Ianuarius, an accountant, to Minerva Augusta and the protecting spirit of the Second Flavian Cohort of Commagenians.

EDCS-11300231.text_edition: Plutoni Rufus praefectus votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300231.translation: To Pluto, Rufus, the prefect, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300231.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Rufus, a prefect, to Pluto.

EDCS-11300233.text_edition: Silvano Rufus praefectus votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300233.translation: To Silvanus, Rufus, the prefect, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300233.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Rufus, a prefect, to Silvanus.

EDCS-11300234.text_edition: Silvano Domestico Aelius Verus armorum custos votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300234.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Aelius Verus, the keeper of arms, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300234.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Aelius Verus, an armorer, to Silvanus Domesticus.

EDCS-11300235.text_edition: Silvano Domestico Aurelia Valentina votum solvit libens
EDCS-11300235.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Aurelia Valentina willingly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-11300235.summary: This inscription records the willing fulfillment of a vow by Aurelia Valentina to Silvanus Domesticus.

EDCS-11300236.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Calpurnius Philomusus [dedicated this].
EDCS-11300236.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Calpurnius Philomusus to Silvanus Domesticus.

EDCS-11300237.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Iulius Crescens [dedicated this].
EDCS-11300237.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Iulius Crescens to Silvanus Domesticus.

EDCS-11300238.text_edition: Silvano Domestico Ulpius Mare ex voto posuit
EDCS-11300238.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Ulpius Mare placed this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11300238.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Silvanus Domesticus, dedicated by Ulpius Mare in fulfillment of a vow, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300241.text_edition: Silvano deo aeterno Quintianus decurio alae votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300241.translation: To Silvanus, the eternal god, Quintianus, a decurion of the cavalry wing, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300241.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Silvanus from Quintianus, a decurion of a cavalry unit, fulfilling a vow, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300242.text_edition: Marti ala I Bosporanorum cui praeest Claudius Sosius praefectus  Silvano Domestico et Mercurio sacrum Aelius Ianuarius posuit
EDCS-11300242.translation: Sacred to Mars, the First Bosporan Cavalry Wing, commanded by Claudius Sosius, its prefect. To Silvanus Domesticus and Mercury, Aelius Ianuarius placed this sacred monument.
EDCS-11300242.summary: This complex votive inscription records dedications to Mars by a military unit (the Ala I Bosporanorum) and to Silvanus Domesticus and Mercury by Aelius Ianuarius, indicating military presence and personal piety, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300243.text_edition: Iovi Turmazgadi cohors II Flavia  Commagenorum equitata sagittariorum cui praeest Marcus Arruntius Agrippinus votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300243.translation: To Jupiter Turmazgas, the Second Flavian Cohort of Commagenians, a cavalry unit of archers commanded by Marcus Arruntius Agrippinus, willingly and deservedly fulfilled its vow.
EDCS-11300243.summary: This is a votive inscription from a Roman military unit, the Cohors II Flavia Commagenorum equitata sagittariorum, dedicating to Jupiter Turmazgas, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300244.text_edition: Veneri sacrum Aelia Flaviae aram animo votum posuit
EDCS-11300244.translation: Sacred to Venus. Aelia Flavia placed this altar with a willing spirit in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11300244.summary: This is a votive inscription for an altar dedicated to Venus by Aelia Flavia, made in fulfillment of a vow, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300245.translation: Titus Va... willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300245.summary: This fragment appears to be part of a votive inscription, likely recording Titus Va... fulfilling a vow, but too little remains to be certain of the monument type or recipient.

EDCS-11300246.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording the name and title of a Roman cavalry prefect, Aelius [Quin]tianus, probably from a monument or public work, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300250.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aelia Asinia annorum LI Marcus Antonius Viator coniugi bene merenti posuit
EDCS-11300250.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. To Aelia Asinia, who lived for 51 years, Marcus Antonius Viator placed this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11300250.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording Marcus Antonius Viator dedicating a tombstone or monument to his deceased wife, Aelia Asinia, who lived to 51 years, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300251.text_edition: A AL Aeli-  XX PAT    annos LXX vixit annos EN fili-
EDCS-11300251.translation: [To the Spirits of the Dead] Aeli- ... lived 70 years. [placed for] his son.
EDCS-11300251.summary: This is a highly fragmented funerary inscription, mentioning an individual who lived for 70 years and the dedication by a parent to a son, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300254.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Respecta Aureli- vixit annos LXXV Iustina X-I
EDCS-11300254.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Respecta, daughter of Aurelius, lived 75 years. Iustina...
EDCS-11300254.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Respecta, daughter of Aurelius, who lived to be 75, with Iustina likely being the dedicant or another family member, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300256.text_edition: Dis Manibus Cornelio Businnio vixit annos X
EDCS-11300256.translation: To the Divine Shades. Cornelius Businnius lived for 10 years.
EDCS-11300256.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young boy named Cornelius Businnius, erected by an unknown dedicant, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300257.text_edition: Dis Manibus Mattavia Laeda vixit annos XXV et Blaste filia Lucius Mattavius Blastus coniugi et filiae bene merentibus posuit
EDCS-11300257.translation: To the Divine Shades. Mattavia Laeda lived for 25 years, and Blaste, her daughter. Lucius Mattavius Blastus placed this for his well-deserving wife and daughter.
EDCS-11300257.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Lucius Mattavius Blastus to his wife, Mattavia Laeda, and their daughter, Blaste, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300258.translation: To the Divine Shades. Quintus, standard-bearer of the ala Aeliana.
EDCS-11300258.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus, a standard-bearer of the Aelian cavalry wing, erected by an unknown dedicant, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300288.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely funerary or dedicatory, set up by an individual named Optatus, with no clear date.

EDCS-11300289.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus. Publius C. C. [placed this].
EDCS-11300289.summary: This is a votive dedication to the god Silvanus Domesticus, offered by Publius C. C., likely in the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300292.text_edition: Deo aeterno Aurelius Maximus decurio coloniae quaestor municipii Apulensis  posuit
EDCS-11300292.translation: To the Eternal God. Aurelius Maximus, decurion of the colony, quaestor of the municipium Apulensis, placed this.
EDCS-11300292.summary: This is a votive inscription to an eternal god, dedicated by Aurelius Maximus, a municipal official from Apulum, likely in the later Imperial period.

EDCS-11300294.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Valerius Iustus |(centurio)
EDCS-11300294.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Gaius Valerius Iustus, centurion.
EDCS-11300294.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Gaius Valerius Iustus, a centurion, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300295.text_edition: Marcus Aurelius Calpurnianus |(centurio) numeri singularium Britannicorum dibus Patronis votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300295.translation: Marcus Aurelius Calpurnianus, centurion of the Numerus Singularum Britannicorum, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Patron Deities.
EDCS-11300295.summary: This is a votive inscription to the Patron Deities, dedicated by Marcus Aurelius Calpurnianus, a centurion from a British auxiliary unit, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300296.text_edition: Silvanis et Silvano Campestribus pro pro salute Boceni Fronto pater p(osuit)
EDCS-11300296.translation: To the Silvani and Silvanus Campestris, for the health of Bocenus, Fronto his father placed this.
EDCS-11300296.summary: This is a votive inscription to the Silvani and Silvanus Campestris, dedicated by a father named Fronto for the health of his son, Bocenus, likely in the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300297.text_edition: Soli invicto sacrum Aelius Iulius solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300297.translation: Sacred to Sol Invictus. Aelius Iulius willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300297.summary: This is a votive inscription to Sol Invictus, offered by Aelius Iulius, likely in the later Imperial period, reflecting the growing cult of the sun god.

EDCS-11300305.text_edition: Numini Aesculapio sic et Hygiae Aurelius Chr=RHestus#Chrestus#CRHESTUS votum libens solvit merito
EDCS-11300305.translation: To the divine power of Aesculapius and likewise to Hygia, Aurelius Chrestus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300305.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow made to the gods Aesculapius and Hygia by Aurelius Chrestus.

EDCS-11300306.text_edition: Aesculapio et Hygiae sacrum Publius Furius Saturninus legatus Augusti pro praetore
EDCS-11300306.translation: Sacred to Aesculapius and Hygia. Publius Furius Saturninus, imperial legate with propraetorian power.
EDCS-11300306.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Aesculapius and Hygia by Publius Furius Saturninus, an imperial legate.

EDCS-11300307.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Publi Furii Saturnini legati Augusti pro praetore Publius Aelius Maximianus
EDCS-11300307.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for the well-being of Publius Furius Saturninus, imperial legate with propraetorian power, Publius Aelius Maximianus [made this].
EDCS-11300307.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Publius Aelius Maximianus for the health and safety of Publius Furius Saturninus, an imperial legate.

EDCS-11300308.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Ulpius Maximinus |(centurio) legionis V Macedonicae Gordianae praepositus numeri singularium peditum Britannicorum votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300308.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Ulpius Maximinus, centurion of the Fifth Macedonian Gordian Legion, commander of the detachment of British singular foot soldiers, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300308.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Ulpius Maximinus, a centurion and commander of a military detachment.

EDCS-11300309.text_edition: quod voverat Caius Caerellius Sabinus legatus Augusti legionis XIII geminae legatus Augusti pro praetore provinciae Raetiae posuit
EDCS-11300309.translation: What Gaius Caerellius Sabinus, imperial legate of the Thirteenth Twin Legion, imperial legate with propraetorian power of the province Raetia, had vowed, he placed.
EDCS-11300309.summary: This inscription records the placement of a vowed dedication by Gaius Caerellius Sabinus, a high-ranking imperial legate in command of a legion and a province.

EDCS-11300310.text_edition: decurialis tribunicius tribunus legionis XII fulminatae praefectus cohortis III Cyrenaicae flamen coloniae praefectus quinquennalis pro Impperatoribus Antonino et Vero Auggustis aedilicius decurio votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300310.translation: Decurial tribunicius, tribune of the Twelfth Fulminata Legion, prefect of the Third Cyrenaic Cohort, flamen of the colony, quinquennial prefect for the Emperors Antoninus and Verus Augusti, aedilicius decurio, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300310.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by an individual holding a long list of military, civil, and religious offices, made on behalf of Emperors Antoninus and Verus.

EDCS-11300311.translation: Decurion of the colony, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11300311.summary: This brief inscription records a dedication or act by a decurion of a colony, done willingly and deservedly.

EDCS-11300314.text_edition: us IL- vixit nnos IL Moesicus  coniugi et fili heres posuit  bene merentibus
EDCS-11300314.translation: [...] lived for 50 years. Moesicus, his heir, placed this for his deserving wife and son.
EDCS-11300314.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument placed by Moesicus, an heir, for his wife and son, who lived for 50 years.

EDCS-11300321.text_edition: Dis Manibus Iulio Secundo exploratori stipendiorum XXXII domo Agrippina vixit annos LV h(eredes) faciendum curaverunt
EDCS-11300321.translation: To the Divine Manes. To Iulius Secundus, an explorer who served 32 years, from Agrippina, who lived 55 years. His heirs took care to have this made.
EDCS-11300321.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument for Iulius Secundus, an explorer with 32 years of service from Agrippina, erected by his heirs after his death at 55 years old.

EDCS-11300331.summary: This very brief inscription likely records a dedication or an act by Decebalus, carried out or facilitated by Scorilo, possibly indicating a king and his envoy or an act 'by' the will of Decebalus 'through' Scorilo.

EDCS-11300338.text_edition: Cereri Augustae sacrum Suriacus Augusti nostri dispensator aurariarum cum suis dedicante Aulo Senecione Contiano viro egregio procuratore III Nonas Ianuarias Laeto II et Ceriale consulibus
EDCS-11300338.translation: Sacred to Ceres Augusta. Suriacus, our emperor's financial administrator of the gold mines, with his household, dedicated this, with Aulus Senecio Contianus, a most distinguished man, procurator, officiating on the Nones of January, during the consulship of Laetus II and Cerialis.
EDCS-11300338.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Ceres Augusta by Suriacus, an imperial financial administrator, and his household, likely from the year 215 AD, under the supervision of a procurator.

EDCS-11300339.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute imperii perpetui Marci Antoni Gordiani Augusti Aurelius Gaius beneficiarius consularis  votum posuit merito
EDCS-11300339.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, for the perpetual welfare of the empire of Marcus Antonius Gordianus Augustus, Aurelius Gaius, a beneficiarius consularis, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300339.summary: This inscription is a votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus by Aurelius Gaius, a consular aide, made for the well-being of the empire and Emperor Gordian, likely dating to the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300340.text_edition: DO sacerdos templum Iovis Optimi Maximi Dolicheni pro se et suorumque sic omnium votum posuit
EDCS-11300340.translation: DO, priest, established the temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus for himself and all of his people, thus fulfilling a vow.
EDCS-11300340.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a temple to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus by a priest named DO, made on behalf of himself and his entire household, as a fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-11300341.text_edition: Fortunae Reduci et Genio numeri Aurelius Redux praefectus numeri M H donum libens posuit
EDCS-11300341.translation: To Fortuna Redux and the Genius of the unit, Aurelius Redux, prefect of the unit M H, willingly placed this gift.
EDCS-11300341.summary: This inscription records a gift to Fortuna Redux and the protecting spirit of a military unit (M H) by its prefect, Aurelius Redux.

EDCS-11300342.text_edition: Dea=AEe#Deae#DAEE Fortunae Salutari Marcus Aurelius Verecundus decurio municipii Apulensis pro salute sua et suorunque sic omnium votum libens merito posuit
EDCS-11300342.translation: To the goddess Fortuna Salutis, Marcus Aurelius Verecundus, decurion of the municipality of Apulum, for his own welfare and that of all his people, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300342.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Fortuna Salutis by Marcus Aurelius Verecundus, a decurion from Apulum, made for the welfare of himself and his household.

EDCS-11300343.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Opelleus Surus votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300343.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Opelleus Surus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300343.summary: This inscription is a votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Opelleus Surus, celebrating the fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-11300345.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Onesimus placed this.
EDCS-11300345.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Silvanus Domesticus by Onesimus.

EDCS-11300346.text_edition: Silvano Silvestri votum fecit Titius votum libens merito
EDCS-11300346.translation: To Silvanus Silvestris, Titius made a vow; he willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300346.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Silvanus Silvestris by Titius.

EDCS-11300348.text_edition: Augusti verna adiutor tabularii dono dedit
EDCS-11300348.translation: The emperor's home-born slave, an assistant in the record office, gave this as a gift.
EDCS-11300348.summary: This inscription records a gift made by an imperial slave, a vernus, who served as an assistant in the tabularium (record office).

EDCS-11300349.text_edition: Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Gaius vixit annos L Aelia Ingenua coniugi et Aelius Gaianus filius bene merenti fecerunt
EDCS-11300349.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Aelius Gaius lived for 50 years. Aelia Ingenua made this for her well-deserving husband, and Aelius Gaianus for his father.
EDCS-11300349.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument for Publius Aelius Gaius, who lived for 50 years, erected by his wife Aelia Ingenua and his son Aelius Gaianus.

EDCS-11300350.text_edition: Dis Manibus Alexandrianus civis Bithynus vixit nnos LXV Tatius Lucius vixit annos XXX Antoninus Iulius Lucilianus fratres patri et fratri et Lucia mater bene merentibus posuerunt
EDCS-11300350.translation: To the Divine Manes. Alexandrianus, a Bithynian citizen, lived 65 years. Tatius Lucius lived 30 years. Antoninus Iulius Lucilianus, and his brothers, along with Lucia, their mother, set this up for their well-deserving father and brother.
EDCS-11300350.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the dedication of a monument by a mother and her sons to their deceased husband/father and son/brother, Alexandrianus and Tatius Lucius.

EDCS-11300351.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aurelius Asclepiades vixit nnos LX Aurelia Auina coniugi
EDCS-11300351.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aurelius Asclepiades lived 60 years. Aurelia Auina for her husband.
EDCS-11300351.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Aurelia Auina to her deceased husband, Aurelius Asclepiades.

EDCS-11300352.text_edition: vixit annos Septimiae Speratae vixit nnos IIII Caius Septimius Constans fratri et uxori eius et nepti bene merentibus faciendum curavit
EDCS-11300352.translation: Septimia Sperata lived 4 years. Gaius Septimius Constans had this made for his well-deserving brother, his wife, and his niece.
EDCS-11300352.summary: This funerary inscription was commissioned by Gaius Septimius Constans for his brother, his brother's wife, and his niece, Septimia Sperata, who died at age four.

EDCS-11300353.text_edition: Dis Manibus Syrillioni aedituo qui vixit annos LXXVIII Liberalis Auggustorum libertus a commentariis et Aelia Victoria patri bene merenti
EDCS-11300353.translation: To the Divine Manes. To Syrillio, sacristan, who lived 78 years. Liberalis, freedman of the Augusti, from the records office, and Aelia Victoria [set this up] for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-11300353.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Liberalis, an imperial freedman, and Aelia Victoria to their father, Syrillio, a sacristan, who lived to 78 years old.

EDCS-11300360.text_edition: Apollini Augusto sacrum Panes Nosetis votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300360.translation: Sacred to Apollo Augustus. Panes, son of Nosetis, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300360.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Apollo Augustus by Panes, son of Nosetis, in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-11300361.translation: Dassius to Deana and the college.
EDCS-11300361.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication by Dassius to a goddess named Deana and a college or association.

EDCS-11300362.text_edition: Dianae Augustae Panes Epicadi qui et Suttius dono dedit
EDCS-11300362.translation: To Diana Augusta. Panes, son of Epicadus, who is also called Suttius, gave this as a gift.
EDCS-11300362.summary: This is a votive inscription, a gift dedicated to Diana Augusta by Panes, son of Epicadus (also known as Suttius).

EDCS-11300363.text_edition: Genio collegii kastelli Baridustarum Seneca Bisonis dono dedit
EDCS-11300363.translation: To the Genius of the college of the fortress of the Baridustae. Seneca, son of Bison, gave this as a gift.
EDCS-11300363.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription from Seneca, son of Bison, to the Genius (protective spirit) of the collegium associated with the fortress of the Baridustae.

EDCS-11300364.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Marci Aurelii Superianus et SuperNatus#Superatus#SUNATUS pro salute sua suorumque votum libens merito posuerunt
EDCS-11300364.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Marcus Aurelius Superianus and Superatus, for their own well-being and that of their kin, willingly and deservedly placed this vow.
EDCS-11300364.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Aurelius Superianus and Superatus for the health and safety of themselves and their families.

EDCS-11300365.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo   GOS Antonius Maximus et filii posuit sic
EDCS-11300365.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Antonius Maximus and his sons placed this as such.
EDCS-11300365.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, placed by Antonius Maximus and his sons.

EDCS-11300371.text_edition: Silvano aram posuit Varro Sceni cot sic promiserat collegio Aelius Be
EDCS-11300371.translation: To Silvanus, Varro Scenius erected an altar, as Aelius Be- had promised to the college.
EDCS-11300371.summary: This inscription records an altar dedicated to the god Silvanus by Varro Scenius, fulfilling a promise made to a college by Aelius Be-, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300372.text_edition: Silvano Augusto sacrum Hermes Myrini votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300372.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Augustus. Hermes Myrinus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300372.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to Silvanus Augustus, made by Hermes Myrinus, in grateful fulfillment of a vow, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300374.text_edition: Silvano Silvestri sacrum SODAN -POS Annaius votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300374.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Silvestris. SODAN -POS Annaius willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300374.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to Silvanus Silvestris by Annaius, who fulfilled a vow willingly and deservedly, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300378.text_edition: Dis Manibus Alexander Gai vixit annos XXXX posuit Gaius filius
EDCS-11300378.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Alexander son of Gaius lived for 40 years. Gaius his son erected this.
EDCS-11300378.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Gaius for his father, Alexander, who lived for 40 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300379.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aurelia Sambu vixit annis XXX Publius Aelius Licinianus coniugi innocentissimae posuit bene merenti
EDCS-11300379.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Sambu lived for 30 years. Publius Aelius Licinianus erected this for his most innocent and well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11300379.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Publius Aelius Licinianus for his innocent and deserving wife, Aurelia Sambu, who lived for 30 years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300389.text_edition: Dis Manibus CENS AVG Publius Anto  Baradus Augusti libertus patri bene merenti posuit
EDCS-11300389.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Anto Baradus, a freedman of Augustus, erected this for his well-deserving father.
EDCS-11300389.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Publius Anto Baradus, an Augusti libertus, for his well-deserving father, suggesting an Imperial date, possibly 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300390.summary: This fragment likely indicates an object or monument associated with the Legio XIII Gemina and a person named Tadius, suggesting a military context from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300393.summary: This brief inscription simply records the name Publius Aelius Terentius, likely part of a larger monument, dedication, or funerary text, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300395.text_edition: Dis Manibus Iuliae Maximae vixit annos LXVI
EDCS-11300395.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Iulia Maxima, who lived for 66 years.
EDCS-11300395.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Iulia Maxima, who lived for 66 years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300397.summary: This brief inscription simply records the name Publius Aelius Terentius, likely part of a larger monument, dedication, or funerary text, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300398.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a father dedicating a monument to his son, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300417.text_edition: Dianae aram posivit sic Martinus votum
EDCS-11300417.translation: Martinus placed this altar to Diana, thus fulfilling his vow.
EDCS-11300417.summary: This inscription records Martinus dedicating an altar to the goddess Diana as a fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-11300418.text_edition: O M N Valerii Primus Ianuarius Διάνῃ
EDCS-11300418.translation: To all the gods, Valerius Primus, Ianuarius, to Diana.
EDCS-11300418.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Valerius Primus and Ianuarius to all the gods and specifically to Diana, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300425.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Aselius Alexander made for the safety or well-being of someone, likely a votive offering.

EDCS-11300429.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Claudius Valentinus V lia posuit
EDCS-11300429.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Claudius Valentinus placed this, in accordance with his vow.
EDCS-11300429.summary: This inscription records Claudius Valentinus dedicating a monument to Jupiter Optimus Maximus in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-11300431.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Bellius Saturninus IIIIvir municipii Apulensis votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300431.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Lucius Bellius Saturninus, quattuorvir of the municipium Apulense, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300431.summary: This inscription records Lucius Bellius Saturninus, a local magistrate of Apulum, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-11300432.text_edition: Libens  votum Libero Patri solvit Paulianus
EDCS-11300432.translation: Paulinus willingly fulfilled his vow to Liber Pater.
EDCS-11300432.summary: This inscription records Paulinus dedicating a monument to the god Liber Pater in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-11300434.text_edition: Marcus Aurelius Maximus flamen municipii Apulensis votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300434.translation: Marcus Aurelius Maximus, flamen of the municipium Apulense, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300434.summary: This inscription records Marcus Aurelius Maximus, a priest of the municipium Apulense, fulfilling a vow, likely to a deity.

EDCS-11300438.summary: This inscription simply records the name Publius Aelius Terentius, likely part of a larger, incomplete text, possibly a funerary marker or identification.

EDCS-11300451.text_edition: Dis Manibus Iulia Magiona vixit annos XXI pater posuit filiae bene merenti hic est sita
EDCS-11300451.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Iulia Magiona lived twenty-one years. Her father placed this for his well-deserving daughter. She is buried here.
EDCS-11300451.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording Iulia Magiona, who died at age 21, dedicated by her father, indicating her burial place.

EDCS-11300457.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription, likely dedicated to or by Titus Aelius.

EDCS-11300459.text_edition: vixit annos  Emeritus NAVS filiae pientissumae sic
EDCS-11300459.translation: [---] lived for years [---]. Emeritus NAVS [dedicated this] to his most devoted daughter, thus [---].
EDCS-11300459.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Emeritus NAVS for his exceptionally devoted daughter, indicating a familial dedication.

EDCS-11300460.text_edition: onis filius vix(it) an(nos) --- et Mel vixit annos LX FIL A
EDCS-11300460.translation: [---] son of [---]onis, lived for [---] years. And Mel lived for 60 years. FIL A[---]
EDCS-11300460.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespans of at least two individuals, including Mel who lived to be 60 years old.

EDCS-11300461.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Aelia  vixit annos i filiae heredes et IV IINA
EDCS-11300461.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aelia lived for one year. The heirs and IV IINA[---] [dedicated this] to their daughter.
EDCS-11300461.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Aelia, who died at the age of one year, set up by her heirs and another individual or group.

EDCS-11300463.text_edition: PIIM AVGVS PO IN vixit annos
EDCS-11300463.translation: PIIM AUGUSTUS PO IN[---] lived for years [---].
EDCS-11300463.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, likely funerary, mentioning an individual named Augustus and their lifespan.

EDCS-11300465.text_edition: vixit annos LXX vixit annos XXXX us vixit annos  Ulpius Castus  bene meritis
EDCS-11300465.translation: [---] lived for 70 years. [---] lived for 40 years. [---]us lived for years [---]. Ulpius Castus [dedicated this] to the well-deserving.
EDCS-11300465.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespans of multiple individuals, dedicated to the well-deserving by Ulpius Castus.

EDCS-11300466.translation: To the Divine Manes. Valerius V[---] Aelius [---].
EDCS-11300466.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes, mentioning individuals with the names Valerius and Aelius.

EDCS-11300471.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription recording that someone lived for four years, and mentioning Publia.

EDCS-11300472.translation: Vivia, his wife, [dedicated this] to her husband Aurelius BII[---].
EDCS-11300472.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Vivia, a wife, to her husband Aurelius.

EDCS-11300481.text_edition: Dis Manibus Quinta Quinti vixit nnos XL Quintus Quinti vixit nnos XXX posuerunt
EDCS-11300481.translation: To the Divine Manes. Quinta, daughter of Quintus, lived for 40 years. Quintus, son of Quintus, lived for 30 years. They erected this.
EDCS-11300481.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes, commemorating Quinta, who lived 40 years, and Quintus, who lived 30 years, both children of a Quintus, and erected by unnamed individuals.

EDCS-11300488.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely a tombstone, mentioning Aurelius Saturninus and possibly a date (Kalends) in the 2nd-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300489.text_edition: rvii filia vixit annos XX Postumius Clemens Tyranni faciendum curavit bene merenti
EDCS-11300489.translation: Daughter of Rvus, she lived 20 years. Postumius Clemens, son of Tyrannus, took care to have this made for the well-deserving.
EDCS-11300489.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Postumius Clemens for a 20-year-old woman, likely his wife or a close relative, indicating her premature death.

EDCS-11300492.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Finitus  vixit annos  filius heres posuit  et Supinus filius annorum
EDCS-11300492.translation: To the Divine Manes. Finitus lived for [---] years. His son, his heir, set this up, and Supinus, his son, aged [---] years.
EDCS-11300492.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Finitus by his son and heir, and also mentioning another son, Supinus, indicating a family burial monument.

EDCS-11300509.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a name label or ownership mark, for a person named Gaius Gemellus.

EDCS-11300528.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum  Carvilius Secundinus vixit annos  Lucius Carvilius Rusticinus praefectus cohortis II Flaviae Bessorum fratri in exemplum piissimo ponendum curavit
EDCS-11300528.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Carvilius Secundinus lived for [---] years. Lucius Carvilius Rusticinus, prefect of the Second Flavian Cohort of Bessians, took care to have this placed for his brother, a most pious example.
EDCS-11300528.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Carvilius Secundinus by his brother, Lucius Carvilius Rusticinus, a military prefect, praising his brother's piety.

EDCS-11300537.text_edition: Candia Maximi vixit annos LXXXX Aelius Talanus Dugionis vixit annos LXX titulum posuit filius patri et matri bene merentibus
EDCS-11300537.translation: Candia, daughter of Maximus, lived 90 years. Aelius Talanus, son of Dugio, lived 70 years. Their son set up this monument for his well-deserving father and mother.
EDCS-11300537.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by a son for his long-lived parents, Candia Maximi (who lived 90 years) and Aelius Talanus Dugionis (who lived 70 years).

EDCS-11300538.text_edition: Dantica Cusiona vixit annos XX titulum posuit mater filiae bene merenti
EDCS-11300538.translation: Dantica Cusiona lived 20 years. Her mother set up this monument for her well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-11300538.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by a mother for her young daughter, Dantica Cusiona, who died at the age of 20.

EDCS-11300539.text_edition: Dis Manibus Sabina Dasmeni vixit annos L posuit Senecio coniugi bene merenti et filii Prinada et Surilla et Surinius et Sanilis posuerunt titulum matri bene merenti
EDCS-11300539.translation: To the Divine Manes. Sabina Dasmeni lived 50 years. Senecio set this up for his well-deserving wife, and her children Prinada, Surilla, Surinius, and Sanilis set up this monument for their well-deserving mother.
EDCS-11300539.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Sabina Dasmeni by her husband Senecio and their four children, commemorating her life of 50 years.

EDCS-11300567.text_edition: Imperatoribus Caesaribus Marco Aurelio Antonino et Lucio Aurelio Commodo Auggustis cohors III Gallorum
EDCS-11300567.translation: To the Emperors Caesars Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Commodus Augusti, the Third Cohort of Gauls.
EDCS-11300567.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the Third Cohort of Gauls to the co-emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla) and Lucius Aurelius Commodus (Geta), dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300568.translation: To the Divine Manes. Valerianus, August.
EDCS-11300568.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription dedicated to Valerianus, potentially mentioning his rank or a month (Augustus, i.e., August) in the 2nd-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300570.summary: This inscription records the name of a cavalry troop, likely from a military diploma or dedication.

EDCS-11300573.text_edition: Soli Invicto pro salutem sic Cai Iuli Valentini conductoris salinarum Iulius Omucio libertus actor posuit
EDCS-11300573.translation: To Sol Invictus, for the well-being of Gaius Iulius Valentinus, contractor of the saltworks, Iulius Omucio, his freedman and agent, set this up.
EDCS-11300573.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Sol Invictus made by a freedman for the health of his patron, who was a saltworks contractor.

EDCS-11300574.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris
EDCS-11300574.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, for the well-being of the Emperor Caesar.
EDCS-11300574.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, made on behalf of an emperor.

EDCS-11300575.text_edition: Dis Manibus Iustina vixit annis LV Aurelia vixit annis s veteranus
EDCS-11300575.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iustina lived for 55 years. Aurelia lived for [...] years, veteran.
EDCS-11300575.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument commemorating Iustina and Aurelia, the latter possibly related to a veteran, though the age of Aurelia is incomplete.

EDCS-11300580.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aurelius Severus decurio vixit annos XXXX Aurelius Qui
EDCS-11300580.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Severus, decurion, lived for 40 years. Aurelius Qui[...]
EDCS-11300580.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument for Aurelius Severus, a decurion, and possibly another individual named Aurelius, whose name is incomplete.

EDCS-11300582.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XIII consuli III patri patriae cohors VIII Raetorum civium Romanorum equitata torquata
EDCS-11300582.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, in his 13th tribunician power, 3rd consulship, father of his country, the Eighth Cohort of Raetians, Roman citizens, cavalry, decorated with torques [set this up].
EDCS-11300582.summary: This inscription is a dedicatory monument to Emperor Hadrian, listing his full titles and honors, erected by the Cohors VIII Raetorum equitata, likely dating to the later part of his reign (around 128-129 CE).

EDCS-11300585.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto
EDCS-11300585.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus.
EDCS-11300585.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus (Caracalla), likely a base for a statue or a monumental inscription.

EDCS-11300590.text_edition: Dianae Augustae sacrum Titus Aelius Crescentianus praefectus cohortis IIII Hispanorum [equitatae Antoni][nia]nae votum solvit libens
EDCS-11300590.translation: Sacred to Diana Augusta. Titus Aelius Crescentianus, prefect of the Fourth Cohort of Hispanian [cavalry, Antoni]niana, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300590.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to Diana Augusta, made by Titus Aelius Crescentianus, a prefect of a cavalry cohort.

EDCS-11300591.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Aelius Honoratus praefectus cohhortis IIII Hispanorum domo Romae v(otum) s(olvit) l(ibens) m(erito)
EDCS-11300591.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Publius Aelius Honoratus, prefect of the Fourth Cohort of Spaniards, from Rome, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300591.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Publius Aelius Honoratus, a prefect of a Spanish cohort, who was from Rome.

EDCS-11300593.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Serenus praefectus cohortis IIII Hispanorum equitatae Gordianae
EDCS-11300593.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Aurelius Serenus, prefect of the Fourth Cohort of Hispanian cavalry, Gordiana [set this up].
EDCS-11300593.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Serenus, a prefect of the Cohors IIII Hispanorum equitata Gordiana.

EDCS-11300594.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Eros Zotici signifer votum libens solvit
EDCS-11300594.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Eros, standard-bearer of Zoticus, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300594.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription by Eros, a standard-bearer, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, indicating a fulfilled vow.

EDCS-11300595.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Iulius Iucundus praefectus cohortis IIII Hispanorum equitatae vN(otum)#v(otum)#N solvit libens merito
EDCS-11300595.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Lucius Julius Jucundus, prefect of the Fourth Cohort of Spanish Cavalry, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300595.summary: This inscription records a vow made and fulfilled by Lucius Julius Jucundus, a cavalry prefect, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-11300596.text_edition: Libero Patri - Aelius Fuscus decurio cohortis IIII Hispanorum equitata voto libens rettulit
EDCS-11300596.translation: To Liber Pater, Aelius Fuscus, decurion of the Fourth Cohort of Spanish Cavalry, willingly rendered his vow.
EDCS-11300596.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Aelius Fuscus, a decurion, to Liber Pater, marking the fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-11300598.text_edition: I MION AVIZ CIN I OALV S VS I M
EDCS-11300598.translation: I MION AVIZ CIN I OALV S VS I M
EDCS-11300598.summary: This inscription is largely illegible or contains nonsensical characters, thus its purpose or meaning cannot be determined.

EDCS-11300602.text_edition: Dis Manibus Ulpius Restitutus vixit annos LV
EDCS-11300602.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Ulpius Restitutus lived 55 years.
EDCS-11300602.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Ulpius Restitutus and stating his age at death.

EDCS-11300626.summary: This short inscription records that Rusticus was the maker or creator of the object on which it is inscribed.

EDCS-11300643.translation: Candidus, from the centuria of Maximus.
EDCS-11300643.summary: This inscription identifies Candidus as belonging to the military unit or 'centuria' commanded by Maximus, likely a centurion.

EDCS-11800566.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno et Deae Suriae Magnae Caelesti pro salute perpetui imperii Romani et legionis XIII geminae FElavius#Flavius#ELAVIUS Barhadadi sacerdos Iovis Dolicheni ad legionem supra scriptam votum libens merito posuit
EDCS-11800566.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus and to the Great Syrian Goddess Caelestis, for the welfare of the eternal Roman Empire and the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, Flavius Barhadadi, priest of Jupiter Dolichenus at the aforesaid legion, willingly and deservedly placed this vow.
EDCS-11800566.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription by Flavius Barhadadi, a priest of Jupiter Dolichenus, to Jupiter Dolichenus and the Great Syrian Goddess Caelestis, for the well-being of the Roman Empire and the Thirteenth Legion Gemina.

EDCS-11800571.text_edition: Dedicatum X Kalendas Iulias Arriano et Papo consulibus
EDCS-11800571.translation: Dedicated on the tenth day before the Kalends of July, with Arrianus and Papus as consuls.
EDCS-11800571.summary: This inscription records the dedication date of a monument, specifically June 22nd, in the year when Arrianus and Papus served as consuls (AD 243).

EDCS-11800573.translation: Fortunate is the Thirteenth Legion Gemina Antoniniana.
EDCS-11800573.summary: This inscription is an acclamation or honorary statement praising the Thirteenth Legion Gemina Antoniniana.

EDCS-12000862.text_edition: Genio publici portorii et Titi Iuli Saturnini conductoris publici portorii Illyrici Maximianus servus vilicus ex privatis pro salute sua suorumque
EDCS-12000862.translation: To the Genius of the public customs and of Titus Julius Saturninus, conductor of the public customs of Illyricum, Maximianus, a slave, the bailiff from private funds, for his own well-being and that of his family.
EDCS-12000862.summary: This is a dedication made by a slave bailiff named Maximianus to the Genius of the public customs and to his master, Titus Julius Saturninus, for the welfare of himself and his family, funded from private resources.

EDCS-12000864.translation: The Thirteenth Legion Gemina, Aurelius Tertius (or Testius).
EDCS-12000864.summary: This inscription records the name Aurelius Tertius (or Testius) associated with the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, likely indicating his service or membership in that legion.

EDCS-12000866.translation: The Thirteenth Legion Gemina, Aurelius Menander.
EDCS-12000866.summary: This inscription records the name Aurelius Menander associated with the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, likely indicating his service or membership in that legion.

EDCS-12000869.text_edition: Deo Invicto Mithrae sacrum  Statorius anus decurio et flamen municipii Septimi Apulensis Invicti templum pro salutem sic sua suorumque pecunia mea feci
